questions.stem: A researcher measures response time on a cognitive task but the equipment malfunctions for any response exceeding 5000 milliseconds, recording those as exactly 5000 ms. Several participants' actual response times exceeded this threshold. Which measure of central tendency would be MOST appropriate for summarizing these data?
options: The mean, because it uses all available data points and is the most efficient estimator
The mode, because it is unaffected by extreme scores or truncation
The median, because it is not distorted by the artificial ceiling on extreme scores
The trimmed mean, because it removes both the highest and lowest scores
questions.answer: The median, because it is not distorted by the artificial ceiling on extreme scores
questions.explanation: When data is truncated or censored (scores above a threshold are recorded at that threshold), the mean would be artificially lowered because extreme scores are compressed. The lesson states: 'When data is skewed, the median is usually better than the mean for representing a typical score because the mean gets distorted by those extreme values.' The same logic applies to truncated data — the median depends only on the rank order of scores, not their exact values, so it remains accurate even when some extreme values are artificially capped. Option A is wrong because the mean would be systematically biased downward by the truncated values. Option B is incorrect because while the mode is unaffected, it only reflects the most frequent value and provides limited information about central tendency in continuous data. Option D is partially reasonable but removes valid low scores unnecessarily and may still be affected if many scores are at the ceiling.
questions.kn_explanation: Equipment malfunction causing censored/truncated data is a data collection methods issue.
questions.quality_comment: Good applied question. The lesson covers median superiority for skewed data, and the logic extends to
questions.suggestedParagraph: ### Skewed Distributions: When Data Piles to One Side
questions.lessonExcerpt: When data is skewed, the median is usually better than the mean for representing a typical score because the mean gets distorted by those extreme values.

questions.stem: A dataset of anxiety scores has a positively skewed distribution with a mean of 35, a standard deviation of 8, and a range from 20 to 75. If each score is converted to a z-score, what will be true about the resulting distribution?
options: The distribution will become approximately normal with a mean of 0 and standard deviation of 1
The distribution will retain its positively skewed shape but will have a mean of 0 and standard deviation of 1
The distribution will become negatively skewed because the transformation reverses the tail direction
The distribution will become leptokurtic because z-score conversions compress extreme values
questions.answer: The distribution will retain its positively skewed shape but will have a mean of 0 and standard deviation of 1
questions.explanation: Converting raw scores to z-scores is a linear transformation that changes the mean to 0 and the standard deviation to 1, but it does NOT change the shape of the distribution. A positively skewed distribution remains positively skewed after z-score conversion. The lesson describes the normal distribution properties (mean, median, and mode equal; 68-95-99 rule) but z-score transformations only produce a normal distribution when the original data are already normally distributed. Option A is a common misconception — z-scores standardize the scale but do not normalize skewed data. Option C is wrong because linear transformations preserve the direction of skew. Option D is wrong because z-scores do not compress extreme values; they merely re-express them on a standardized scale.
questions.kn_explanation: Linear transformations of scores and their effects on distribution properties (mean, SD, skewness) fall under descriptive statistics/analytic methods.
questions.quality_comment: Tests a very common EPPP misconception. The lesson content discusses normal distributions and skewed distributions but
questions.suggestedParagraph: ### The Normal Distribution: The Ideal Bell Curve
questions.lessonExcerpt: {{M}}If you measure the wait times for appointments at a large clinic and get a normal distribution with a mean of 15 minutes and standard deviation of 5 minutes, you know that about 68% of clients wa

questions.stem: How does age function as a moderator variable in psychological research?
options: It directly affects the dependent variable
It changes the strength or direction of the relationship between variables
It is an extraneous variable that must be controlled
It serves as the independent variable in studies
questions.answer: It changes the strength or direction of the relationship between variables
questions.explanation: Age as a moderator variable influences how the independent variable affects the dependent variable, making it the correct answer. The other options mischaracterize the role of age in research.
questions.kn_explanation: This KN code applies as it relates to the role of age in correlation strength.
questions.quality_comment: The question is challenging and requires a deep understanding of moderator variables.
questions.lessonExcerpt: A moderator variable changes the strength or direction of the relationship between your independent and dependent variables.

questions.stem: When ranking scores based on percentiles, which of the following statements is true?
options: Percentiles indicate the percentage of scores that fall below a specific score.
Percentiles provide the exact score corresponding to a specific percentile rank.
Percentiles can only be used with normally distributed data.
Percentiles are the same as standard scores.
questions.answer: Percentiles indicate the percentage of scores that fall below a specific score.
questions.explanation: The correct answer accurately describes what percentiles represent in a dataset. The other options either misinterpret the concept of percentiles or incorrectly limit their applicability.
questions.kn_explanation: This question is best aligned with KN54 because it tests Design of case, correlational, quasi-experimental, and experimental studies.
questions.quality_comment: The question is well-constructed and tests knowledge of descriptive statistics.
questions.suggestedParagraph: ### Scales of Measurement: How We Quantify Experience
questions.lessonExcerpt: A percentile rank tells you the percentage of people in the reference group who scored at or below a particular score. If your client receives a percentile rank of 82, it means 82% of people in the comparison group scored the same or lower.

questions.stem: A researcher collects annual income data from a sample of psychologists but many participants at the high end report only that they earn '$200,000 or more' rather than providing exact figures. When selecting a measure of central tendency for this dataset, the researcher should use the:
options: Mean, because income is a ratio-level variable
Mode, because it is not affected by extreme values
Median, because the mean would be distorted by the inability to calculate exact values for high earners
Geometric mean, because income data is always positively skewed
questions.answer: Median, because the mean would be distorted by the inability to calculate exact values for high earners
questions.explanation: When data is censored (exact values unknown above a threshold), the mean cannot be accurately computed because it requires all exact values. The median, which depends only on the rank order of scores, can be determined as long as the median falls below the censoring threshold. The lesson establishes that 'when data is skewed, the median is usually better than the mean for representing a typical score because the mean gets distorted by those extreme values.' Income is specifically cited as an example of positively skewed data where extreme values pull the mean. Censored data at the upper end compounds this problem since exact values cannot even be assigned. The mode would lose too much information. The geometric mean is not discussed in the lesson content.
questions.kn_explanation: This question tests selection of appropriate central tendency measures when data is censored/truncated, drawing on descriptive statistics knowledge.
questions.quality_comment: Good applied question. The lesson covers skewed distributions and median as preferred measure but
questions.suggestedParagraph: ### Skewed Distributions: When Data Piles to One Side
questions.lessonExcerpt: When data is skewed, the median is usually better than the mean for representing a typical score because the mean gets distorted by those extreme values.
